elements.sent-2411-001: Burn injuries present a significant global health challenge.
elements.sent-2411-002: Among the most severe long-term consequences are contractures, which can lead to functional impairments and disfigurement.
elements.sent-2411-003: Understanding and predicting the evolution of post-burn wounds is essential for developing effective treatment strategies.
elements.sent-2411-004: Traditional mathematical models, while accurate, are often computationally expensive and time-consuming, limiting their practical application.
elements.sent-2411-005: Recent advancements in machine learning, particularly in deep learning, offer promising alternatives for accelerating these predictions.
elements.sent-2411-006: This study explores the use of a deep operator network (DeepONet), a type of neural operator, as a surrogate model for finite element simulations, aimed at predicting post-burn contraction across multiple wound shapes.
elements.sent-2411-007: A DeepONet was trained on three distinct initial wound shapes, with enhancement made to the architecture by incorporating initial wound shape information and applying sine augmentation to enforce boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-008: The performance of the trained DeepONet was evaluated on a test set including finite element simulations based on convex combinations of the three basic wound shapes.
elements.sent-2411-009: The model achieved an $R^2$ score of $0.99$, indicating strong predictive accuracy and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-010: Moreover, the model provided reliable predictions over an extended period of up to one year, with speedups of up to 128-fold on CPU and 235-fold on GPU, compared to the numerical model.
elements.sent-2411-011: These findings suggest that DeepONets can effectively serve as a surrogate for traditional finite element methods in simulating post-burn wound evolution, with potential applications in medical treatment planning.
elements.sent-2411-012: Keywords: Operator learning, neural networks, surrogate model, wound modeling.
elements.sent-2411-013: Skin is the largest organ of the human body, and its weight is about 15 percent of the total body weight.
elements.sent-2411-014: Roughly speaking, skin consists of three layers: the epidermis (top layer), the dermis, and the subcutis.
elements.sent-2411-015: The subcutis is connected to underlying fat, organs, and muscles.
elements.sent-2411-016: If only the epidermis is damaged, then full healing will occur by the proliferation and migration of keratinocytes, which populate the epidermis.
elements.sent-2411-017: If deeper layers, such as the dermis or even the subcutis, are damaged, then the repair mechanism entails the regeneration of the dermis through collagen secretion by fibroblasts and the regeneration of the small blood vessel (capillary) network by revascularization.
elements.sent-2411-018: Serious burn injuries are characterized by damage on multiple skin (dermal) layers and over large areas of the human body.
elements.sent-2411-019: The healing of these large burn injuries often goes along with {\em dermal contraction and/or {\em hypertrophy .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: These mechanisms are mainly caused by the (strain-induced) differentiation of fibroblasts to myofibroblasts.
elements.sent-2411-021: While fibroblasts produce collagen type 1, the natural collagen in undamaged (embryonic) skin, myofibroblasts mainly secrete collagen type 3 at a large turnover per unit of time.
elements.sent-2411-022: Furthermore, myofibroblasts exert pulling forces, eventually making the wound area contract.
elements.sent-2411-023: At the same time, the epidermis is closed by the regeneration and migration of keratinocytes.
elements.sent-2411-024: Clinicians refer to the injury as a {\em wound during epidermal healing, after which the injury is referred to as a {\em scar .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Although epidermal closure and dermal contraction influence each other, their exact interplay is still unclear, and therefore, we will not consider this interaction in the current study.
elements.sent-2411-026: Dermal contraction may reduce the dermal pliability to such an extent that joints lose their mobility, and patients can no longer exercise or perform daily routines.
elements.sent-2411-027: In these cases, the occurred contraction is referred to as a {\em contracture .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Another side effect that patients with serious burn injuries endure is hypertrophy of scars.
elements.sent-2411-029: This hypertrophy is characterized by a changed dermal topography, which generally changes the appearance of the skin.
elements.sent-2411-030: While serious burn injuries often resulted in patient death in the past, nowadays, more and more patients survive severe burns.
elements.sent-2411-031: This is why current health care has shifted its focus from survival to the increase of quality of life of the survived patients.
elements.sent-2411-032: Therefore, clinical treatments are directed towards changing cellular behavior such that contraction and deposition of type 3 collagen are mitigated as much as possible.
elements.sent-2411-033: This mitigation is often achieved through methods such as dressings, clothing, skin grafting (skin transplantation by depositing epidermal parts from other body parts on the injured part), and splinting.
elements.sent-2411-034: Many scientific studies, both clinical and laboratory, have been carried out and are ongoing to improve health care for burn injuries.
elements.sent-2411-035: In order to improve health care, one aims to steer the behavior of involved cells, and therefore, it is crucially important to understand the chain of biophysical mechanisms responsible for the evolution of post-burned skin.
elements.sent-2411-036: This knowledge needs to be validated using laboratory experiments and clinical observations, which represent data in numbers and patterns, and hence, the developed theory needs to be quantified.
elements.sent-2411-037: This quantification proceeds by using mathematical models based on either stochastic (random) processes and partial differential equations (continuum models) or by combinations of the two.
elements.sent-2411-038: To this extent, the current paper involves mathematical models in terms of partial differential equations (PDEs).
elements.sent-2411-039: These equations contain input parameters, such as elasticity and cell division rates, that are unknown on forehand due to patient-to-patient variations.
elements.sent-2411-040: For this reason, one simulation run cannot be deemed reliable in general, and therefore, one merely aims at predicting probabilities of clinical successes depending on the therapy.
elements.sent-2411-041: This can only be done by sampling the input parameters from prior probability distributions so that posterior probability distributions for the output parameters, such as the extent of wound contraction or strain energy, can be estimated.
elements.sent-2411-042: One of the bottlenecks in making current simulation tools applicable to clinical practice is the relatively long simulation time required to run one sample.
elements.sent-2411-043: To address this, neural network-based frameworks are being developed, where a regression-like model links input parameters to the output parameters of interest.
elements.sent-2411-044: Ideally, the resulting model can generate simulation runs at very large speedups compared to classical numerical simulations.
elements.sent-2411-045: The first neural networks to reproduce post-burn contraction finite element simulations in one and two dimensions have been introduced in~\cite{Egberts2022 and~\cite{Egberts2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: These studies indicate high fidelity and high-speed reproduction of the finite element simulations.
elements.sent-2411-047: However, a limitation of these works is that all simulations were performed on a fixed spatial domain, meaning the wound's size and shape remain constant.
elements.sent-2411-048: This is not very representative of real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-049: In this study, we propose using neural operators to generate a surrogate model that reproduces the results of finite-element simulations, predicting post-burn wound evolution over time.
elements.sent-2411-050: Neural operators are a class of deep learning architectures with the primary application of learning surrogate maps for the solution operators of PDEs.
elements.sent-2411-051: Specifically, we will focus on deep operator networks (DeepONets) for learning operators accurately and efficiently from a relatively small dataset~\cite{lu_deeponet_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-052: In particular, we aim to learn the solution operator of the two-dimensional morphoelastic model, which describes post-burn skin evolution, thus accurately reproducing the finite element predictions.
elements.sent-2411-053: A key aspect of this research is incorporating multiple initial wound shapes.
elements.sent-2411-054: We train the neural operator network on a set of basic wound shapes, and evaluate its fidelity using convex combinations of the basic wound shapes as input.
elements.sent-2411-055: In this way, our research aims to extend the works \cite{Egberts2022 and \cite{Egberts2023 .}}

elements.sent-2411-059: This sections details the methodology, describing both the numerical simulations and the machine learning models employed.
elements.sent-2411-060: We first present the mathematical model for post-burn contraction, starting with the general system of equations and then providing a more detailed description of the relevant biological and mechanical components.
elements.sent-2411-061: Furthermore, we give the boundary conditions, initial conditions, and numerical solver, and introduce an important measure of contraction.
elements.sent-2411-062: The general morphoelastic model for post-burn contraction was developed by Koppenol \cite{koppenol2017mathematical , who used the theory of morphoelasticity developed by Hall \cite{hall2008modelling to incorporate the formation of long-term deformations (contraction) into the dermal layer of the skin.}}

elements.sent-2411-064: The mechanical components are the dermal layer displacement $(\mathbf{u )$, the dermal layer displacement velocity $(\mathbf{v )$, and the effective strain $(\pmb{\varepsilon )$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-156: We also call $\mathcal{L (\theta)$ the loss function, which here is based on the mean squared error (MSE).}

elements.sent-2411-187: We propose a machine learning model, based on the DeepONet architecture, for predicting post-burn contraction over time.

elements.sent-2411-189: It consists of a branch and trunk network, both of which are simple MLPs with three hidden layers, containing 50 neurons per layer.
elements.sent-2411-190: The branch takes as input five parameters from the morphoelastic model describing wound contraction: the cell diffusion constant $D_F$, the chemotactic constant $\chi_F$, the signaling diffusion constant $D_c$, the cell differentiation rate $k_F$, and the half-maximum cell division enhancement rate $a_c^I$.

elements.sent-2411-195: The output of the trunk network is a vector $(c_1, \dots, c_{p ) \in \mathbb{R ^{p}$.}}

elements.sent-2411-269: First, we define the performance measures used to determine the metrics in \Cref{tab:performance .}

elements.sent-2411-271: We include the goodness of fit ($R^2$) statistic, the average relative root mean squared error (aRRMSE), and the average relative error (aRelErr): \begin{equation}R^2 = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{t=1}^2 \left[1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n \left(u_t^{(i)}-\hat{u}_t^{(i)}\right)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^n \left(u_t^{(i)}-\bar{u}_t\right)^2}\right], \label{eq:R2} aRRMSE = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{t=1}^2 \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^n \left(u_t^{(i)}-\hat{u}_t^{(i)}\right)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^n \left(u_t^{(i)}-\bar{u}_t\right)^2}} \label{eq:aRRMSE}, aRelErr = \label{eq:aRelErr} \frac{1}{2} \sum_{t=1}^{2} \frac{1}{n_t} \sum_{i=1}^{n} I(u_t^{(i)} \neq 0) \cdot \left| \frac{u_t^{(i)} - \hat{u}_t^{(i)}}{u_t^{(i)}} \right|,\end{equation} where.
elements.figure-2411-011: \caption{Performance of the proposed DeepONet compared to four other setups (see \Cref{fig:architecture} for a visualization). Boldface indicates the best performance} \textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}lccccc@{}} \textbf{\begin{tabular}[c]{@{}l@{}}Performance metric\end{tabular}} \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}c@{}}No sine aug. and no initial shape info (Case 1)\end{tabular} \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}c@{}}Initial shape info to branch (Case 2) \end{tabular} \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}c@{}}Initial shape info to trunk (Case 3)\end{tabular} \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}c@{}}Sine augmentation (Case 4)\end{tabular} \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}c@{}}Sine aug. and initial shape info to trunk (Proposed model)\end{tabular} $R^2$ $0.90040$ $0.89613$ $0.99155$ $0.89083$ $\mathbf{0.99437}$ aRRMSE $0.31560$ $0.32229$ $0.09166$ $0.33032$ $\mathbf{0.07497}$ aRelErr $0.12160$ $0.11673$ $0.04432$ $0.15050$ $\mathbf{0.03429}$ \end{tabular}} \label{tab:performance}
elements.sent-2411-272: A value of $R^2 = 1$ indicates perfect predictions, whereas $R^2 = 0$ indicates that the model is not better than always returning the expected values.
elements.sent-2411-273: For our proposed DeepONet, we find $R^2 = 0.99$.
elements.sent-2411-274: This shows that the model can accurately predict dermal displacement.
elements.sent-2411-275: Furthermore, we find an aRRMSE of $0.08$, showing excellent performance according to Despotovic et al. \cite{despotovic2016evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-276: Lastly, the aRelErr is found to be only $0.03$, which further indicates that the model can excellently reproduce the finite element simulations.
elements.sent-2411-277: We observe that, compared to all variations, the proposed model gives the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-278: The "skeleton" DeepONet, where the model does not have any information about the initial wound shape and no sine augmentation is applied (case 1), performs significantly worse.
elements.sent-2411-280: Adding initial wound shape info in the form of the quadruple $(y_{cut , x_m, y_m, x_{cut )$ to the branch (case 2), even gives slightly worse performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-281: The largest gain is when this initial shape info is added to the trunk (case 3).
elements.sent-2411-282: Here we see a decrease of $71 \%$ in the aRRMSE and a decrease of $63.6\%$ in the aRelErr, compared to case 1.
elements.sent-2411-283: This suggests that initial shapes should be input to the trunk, as this component of the network is responsible for learning the basis functions.
elements.sent-2411-284: Improved representation of basis functions often leads to a more accurate approximation of the solution.
elements.sent-2411-285: It is also the most logical choice because the initial shape info is more closely related to the spatial coordinates, which are also fed into the trunk, rather than to the parameters, which are input to the branch.
elements.sent-2411-286: Case 4, where the skeleton DeepONet only has the addition of the sine augmentation blocks, is the worst performing one with $R^2 = 0.89$, $aRRMSE=0.33$, and $aRelErr = 0.15$.
elements.sent-2411-287: From this, we conclude that the network cannot sufficiently learn the dermal displacements without knowledge of the initial shape of the wound.
elements.sent-2411-288: Additionally, sine augmentation provides an improvement in performance only when the model already performs sufficiently without it.
elements.sent-2411-289: Indeed, if we combine cases 3 and 4, we see that our proposed model overall results in the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-290: So far, we have taken $t\in[0,100]$ days for all predictions to save time when generating data using numerical simulations.
elements.sent-2411-291: However, we are interested to see if our final DeepONet is also capable of predicting $t\in[0,365]$ days.
elements.sent-2411-292: We observe that the predicted RSAW is linearly increasing after $t = 100$ days, and no asymptotic value is reached, which means the network predicts a scar that continues to grow in size.
elements.sent-2411-293: This indicates the need for adding data to the training set with $t_{end = 365$.}
elements.sent-2411-294: We experiment with different data sampling strategies.
elements.sent-2411-295: Specifically, we compare the effects of incorporating data from additional finite element simulations, to drawing more data points from existing samples.
elements.sent-2411-296: Practically, this comparison reflects the difference between including data on more burn patients versus collecting more data per patient.
elements.sent-2411-297: For each case, we initialize the network with the learned parameters from the final DeepONet trained on $t\in [0,100]$, as a good initial guess to speed up the training.
elements.sent-2411-298: In this way, the network embeds prior knowledge, instead of having to start learning from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-299: Note that both result in the same number of additional data points (30\,000).
elements.sent-2411-300: In both the best and worst cases, we observe that scenario 2 performs better.
elements.sent-2411-301: Sampling more data per existing finite element run does not lead to much improvement.
elements.sent-2411-302: In fact, in~\Cref{fig:best_worst_rsaw_sampling (a), we even observe a decrease compared to selecting 10 time steps.}
elements.sent-2411-303: Adding new, unique data results in a performance increase.
elements.sent-2411-304: In the best case, the predicted RSAW at $t=365$ is now only 0.47\,\% higher than the target.
elements.sent-2411-305: In the worst case, this is 0.6\,\%.
elements.sent-2411-306: It exposes the network to a wider variety of features, which can enable it to generalize better.
elements.sent-2411-307: On the other hand, introducing more data per existing samples can introduce redundancy, as the network is repeatedly exposed to the same patterns.
elements.sent-2411-308: Since these patterns were already learned in the original data, additional exposure does not result in significant improvement and can lead to overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-309: This means that the network becomes too specialized in recognizing the patterns in the training data, reducing its ability to generalize to new, unseen data.

The legend denotes the number of finite element simulations added to the training set, and the number of time steps sampled per simulation} \label{fig:best_worst_rsaw_sampling}
elements.sent-2411-310: In this study, all computations were performed on the DelftBlue supercomputer, provided by the Delft High Performance Computing Centre \cite{DHPC2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-311: For the finite element simulations, we used a single node equipped with an Intel Xeon Gold 6248R CPU, using one core and 2 GB of memory.
elements.sent-2411-312: Executing the finite element-based model for a one-year prediction required, on average, 4.7 minutes of computing time per sample.
elements.sent-2411-313: In contrast, using the same computational resources, the DeepONet predicted the displacement field in an average of 2.2 seconds per sample, resulting in a speedup of about 128 times.
elements.sent-2411-314: Furthermore, the DeepONet has the advantage that it can be evaluated on a GPU.
elements.sent-2411-315: Using one NVIDIA A100 GPU on DelftBlue, the average prediction time per sample decreased to 1.2 seconds, which gives an additional speedup of almost two times.
elements.sent-2411-316: This shows that, in addition to being effective, DeepONets can serve as fast surrogates for traditional finite element methods in simulating post-burn contraction.
elements.sent-2411-317: In \cite{Egberts2023 , MLPs were trained for the much simpler task of predicting the RSAW and wound boundary over time, given 25 parameters from the 2D morphoelastic model.}
elements.sent-2411-318: Here, a total of 21 spatial points were used to describe a fixed initial wound shape.
elements.sent-2411-319: Since this differs from our approach of predicting the entire displacement field over time for multiple wound shapes, even shapes that were not 'seen' in the training data, a direct one-to-one comparison is not possible.
elements.sent-2411-320: The CPU time needed for our DeepONet to evaluate only the nodes on the wound boundary (100 in total) for each $t\in[0,365]$ is, on average, 0.27 seconds per sample.
elements.sent-2411-321: If we compare this to the execution time of the numerical model, we find a speedup of about 1044 times.
elements.sent-2411-322: This means that, per sample evaluation, many forward passes need to be made until the network has processed all grid points in the domain, for each $t\in [0,365]$.
elements.sent-2411-323: For example, one sample from the convex test used for one-year predictions contains an average of $180\,010$ points $(t,x,y)$.
elements.sent-2411-324: Consequently, with a batch size of $50$, the model has to be evaluated approximately $3600$ times per sample.
elements.sent-2411-325: This is different from the previous approaches, where the MLP had to be evaluated only once to predict all quantities for each case.
elements.sent-2411-326: However, the flexibility regarding geometry realized by our DeepONet is a significant advancement, while still achieving a speedup of about 128 on CPU and 235 on GPU.
elements.sent-2411-327: We trained a DeepONet on three different initial wound shapes to accurately predict the dermal displacement across the entire domain over time.
elements.sent-2411-328: The DeepONet consists of a branch and trunk network.
elements.sent-2411-329: The branch network takes as input five parameters from the morphoelastic model describing burn injuries: the cell diffusion constant, the chemotactic constant, the signaling diffusion constant, the cell differentiation rate, and the half-maximum cell division enhancement rate.
elements.sent-2411-330: The trunk takes as input a coordinate $(t,x,y)$ where we want to evaluate the solution, along with information about the initial wound shape and size given by the quadruple $(y_{cut , x_m, y_m, x_{cut )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-331: The final DeepONet output is the result of a dot product of the branch and trunk network outputs, such that the five input parameters are mapped to the displacement fields for 100 post-burn days.
elements.sent-2411-332: We found that the predictions closely matched targets, demonstrating good generalization.
elements.sent-2411-333: The scatter plots in \Cref{fig:true_vs_predicted_conv confirm this, showing a strong concentration around $y=x$ with minimal outliers.}
elements.sent-2411-334: The model slightly underperformed on mid-range $y$-displacements, but excelled in predicting both small and large displacements.
elements.sent-2411-335: The error analysis in \Cref{fig:AE showed a gradual increase in absolute error over time, peaking around day 90.}
elements.sent-2411-336: However, the mean plus standard deviation of the error never exceeds 3\%, which corresponds to deviations on the order of tenths of millimeters.
elements.sent-2411-337: From a practical medical perspective, these are very accurate predictions.
elements.sent-2411-338: The final model, incorporating initial wound shape info and sine augmentation, achieved the best performance with an $R^2$ score of $0.99$, an aRRMSE of $0.08$, and an aRelErr of $0.03$.
elements.sent-2411-339: This confirms the model's strong generalization abilities.
elements.sent-2411-340: The results support that initial shape info should be input to the trunk, and sine augmentation enhances performance when the model is already well-tuned.
elements.sent-2411-341: We expanded the training set with 50 new finite element simulations, where $t_{end =365$.}
elements.sent-2411-342: We compared the RSAW curves for two scenarios: one where 50 finite element simulations were added to the training set, with 30 time steps sampled in $t \in (100, 365]$, and another where 150 runs were added, with 10 time steps each.
elements.sent-2411-343: We found that adding new, unique data (the first scenario) results in the greatest improvement.
elements.sent-2411-344: Practically, these findings suggest that including more patients with less data per patient is preferable to having fewer patients with more data per patient.
elements.sent-2411-345: This is likely because introducing new information provides a more varied learning experience, whereas additional data points from existing samples tend to add redundancy, offering less benefit.
elements.sent-2411-346: The training encompassed multiple initial wound shapes, with the network demonstrating strong generalization on convex combinations of these basic shapes.
elements.sent-2411-347: Furthermore, by adding a limited number of samples from one-year finite element simulations into the training set, the network achieved reasonable predictions of the RSAW over the entire year.
elements.sent-2411-348: We anticipate that including more data with $t_{end = 365$ will further enhance prediction accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-349: While this speedup is lower than that achieved in~ \cite{Egberts2022, Egberts2023 for MLP models on a fixed geometry, the proposed DeepONet allows for geometrical flexibility that was not possible with the MLPs employed in previous works.}
elements.sent-2411-350: A promising direction for further research is to investigate the application of more complex and realistic initial wound geometries, where, for example, no symmetry in $x$ and $y$ is present.
elements.sent-2411-351: One possible approach is to include convolutional layers before the trunk network, allowing for the direct input of an image of the initial wound shape, thereby increasing the model applicability.
elements.sent-2411-352: Additionally, an alternative to sine augmentation is to penalize discrepancies between the predicted and true displacements on the domain boundary within the loss function, adopting a PINN-like approach for softer enforcement of the boundary conditions \cite{raissi_physics-informed_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-353: Further integration of DeepONets with PINNs can also ensure that all predicted displacements adhere more closely to the governing PDEs, which now is not necessarily the case \cite{wang2021learning .}
elements.sent-2411-354: This could solve potential discrepancies between the predictions and the underlying physical laws, leading to more reliable outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-355: This approach could provide a more comprehensive and applicable model; however, it would likely introduce larger variability in the solutions, since some parameters (e.g., collagen) are particularly sensitive.
elements.sent-2411-356: This would present an interesting opportunity to test whether the model can manage this increased complexity.
elements.sent-2411-357: Consequently, the architecture may need to be extended or improved.
